schools.summary: Matthew Henry: 'Few and evil were his days' — a degenerate son of David's house who chose Israel's idolatry without even the northern kings' excuse of state.
Keil and Delitzsch: Ahaz's reign is a decisive turn into open apostasy, swiftly punished by the Syro-Israelite defeat.
Gill reconciles the Pekah synchronism and marks the folly of an idolatry that served no interest of state.
Clarke surveys the whole chapter — idolatry, the siege, the Assyrian alliance, the Damascus altar, and the succession of Hezekiah.
Ellicott emends the age to twenty-five with the versions; Keil and Delitzsch expound 'the way of the kings of Israel' as including Baal-worship.
Clarke defends the Masoretic 'twenty,' since an early fatherhood is possible in the East.
Gill notes only that Ahaz reigned sixteen years, as long as his father Jotham.

debates.question: What was the age of Ahaz at his accession?

sides.position: twenty-five, emending with the LXX, Syriac, and Arabic of 2 Chronicles 28:1
twenty, as the Masoretic text stands — an early fatherhood being possible in the East

themes: the way of the kings of Israel

schools.summary: Ellicott: the passing through fire is a real child-sacrifice to Molech; Ahaz the first such king of Judah, driven to it as a desperate resource.
Wesley: an oblation consumed as a burnt offering in heathen imitation; Clarke refers to the Levitical laws against giving seed to Molech.
Gill: Ahaz aped the calf-worship of Israel without even their motive of state, an idolatry against his own interest.
Ellicott distinguishes 'high places,' 'hills,' and the shady 'green tree' by their Hebrew prepositions; Keil and Delitzsch turn the verse toward the invasion that follows.
Gill: no earlier king of Judah so worshipped by his own example; Ahaz likely sacrificed to idols where the people sacrificed to the true God.
Ellicott aligns the siege with Isaiah 7; the allies sought to enthrone the son of Tabeal, but the fortified city held.
Clarke sets Isaiah's consoling word here; Wesley: the siege failed only because God's grace undertook Ahaz's protection.
Gill: Rezin and Pekah were moved by the Lord to chastise Ahaz for his idolatry.
Matthew Henry: those who lose their religion can keep nothing; Ahaz, unable to ask God's help, turns to Assyria.
Ellicott presses the Syria/Edom textual variant: reading 'Edom,' Rezin freed a subject Edom against Judah.
Gill rehearses Elath's tangled history and keeps the received text, declining Le Clerc's emendation 'without authority.'
Clarke refers to Elath's earlier recovery under Azariah.

debates.question: Did Rezin restore Elath to Syria or to Edom?

sides.position: to Edom — reading 'Edomites' with the Hebrew margin, LXX, and Vulgate; Rezin freed a subject Edom
to Syria — keeping the received text; Rezin expelled the Jews and planted a Syrian colony

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'which are assailing me'; an unreasoning panic drove Judah to throw itself on Assyria's protection.
Clarke unfolds the vassal's formula; Wesley: the sin was its own punishment, impoverishing and enslaving king and people.
Gill identifies the Assyrian king addressed, leaving the message's weight to the others.
Gill: the treasure was what Uzziah and Jotham had stored, the older wealth having been plundered by Jehoash.
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott: Tiglath took Damascus and slew Rezin as Amos foretold; the monuments record Ahaz's tribute in 734 B.C.
Ellicott confirms from the inscriptions the two-year siege, Rezin's death and impalement of his officers, and locates Kir in Mesopotamia.
Clarke: Assyria 'distressed him, but strengthened him not' — a costly alliance with meagre and partial return.
Gill notes simply that the king of Assyria complied with Ahaz's request.
Keil and Delitzsch describe the visit of homage; Ellicott sees a trifling virtuoso charmed by a Damascus altar, and names Urijah the 'faithful witness' of Isaiah 8.
Clarke: an idolatrous altar meant for the gods of Damascus; Urijah's compliance mean and sinful.
Matthew Henry: a bolder stroke against religion; the eye-led worship of idolaters set against worship by faith.
Gill notes the 160-mile journey to flatter the Assyrian overlord.
Gill: Urijah built the altar exactly, though against God's express command fixing the altar's form and matter — a timeserver currying favour.
Matthew Henry: the priest prostituted his office and betrayed his trust, willingly serving an idolatrous king.
Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative to the king's offering and the moving of the old altar.
Ellicott: the versions read 'went up thereon' — Ahaz, like Uzziah, personally usurped the priestly office of sacrifice.
Wesley: he offered not to God but to the Syrian idols to whom the altar was appropriated.
Gill: the king liked the altar, made exactly to the pattern he had sent.
Ellicott: a thank-offering that on the surface looks offered to Jehovah — Ahaz the syncretist joining the LORD's worship to foreign cults.
Wesley: the offerings were diverted to false gods, the forms of true worship given to idols in heathen imitation.
Gill: the burnt and meal offerings went together by the law, a form the heathen aped.

debates.question: To whom did Ahaz offer on the new altar?

sides.position: to Jehovah, on the narrative's surface — a syncretist joining the LORD's worship to foreign cults
to the Syrian idols, the forms of true worship diverted to false gods

schools.summary: Ellicott clarifies the maneuver over precedence: Ahaz drew the old altar back and fixed it on the north side of his new one.
Clarke: Ahaz conformed the house to Damascus to honour Syria's gods; Wesley: a bolder stroke against religion than the worst of kings.
Gill identifies Solomon's altar and its station before the Lord at the temple's front.
Matthew Henry: man's invention at length jostles out God's institution — those who will not make God their all make nothing of him.
Ellicott and Keil weigh the readings of 'to enquire by' and the ritual precision of the daily offerings; the 'great altar' is the larger Syrian one.
Wesley: Ahaz pretends to advance the old altar above its institution — 'to overdo is to underdo.'
Gill: Urijah ought not to be commanded by the king in matters of worship.

debates.question: What did Ahaz mean by 'the brasen altar shall be for me to enquire by'?

sides.position: for consulting God (Rashi) — a reserved altar for seeking God's will by sacrifice and prayer
'I will consider what to do with it' — a mere deferral

schools.summary: Gill: 'like king, like priest, both apostates and idolaters' — the corruption of throne and sanctuary is now one guilt.
Matthew Henry: the base compliance of a poor-spirited priest with a presumptuous king; miserable when reprovers become servants of sin.
Keil and Delitzsch make the verse the hinge to the stripping of the bronze furniture.
Gill: the carved borders of the bases cut off and the lavers removed, perhaps to melt the brass, perhaps to spoil the work.
Matthew Henry: a spite against the priests' washing; obstructing their purifying is a great prejudice to religion.
Keil and Delitzsch record the mutilation of the bronze sea and stands without a stated motive.
Ellicott and Keil take it as a covered stand for the king on holy days, with his outer entry; 'turned' is not 'robbed of ornaments.'
Wesley owns the covert's use is now unknown; Clarke reports Jarchi's covert way, removed lest the Assyrian covet the sacred vessels.
Gill: the change signified that Ahaz would frequent the house of God no more.
Matthew Henry: turning the temple approach aside to please a neighbour is going downhill to ruin.

debates.question: What was 'the covert for the sabbath,' and why did Ahaz remove it?

sides.position: a covered stand or hall for the king when he visited the Temple on sabbaths and feasts, altered to appease Assyria
a covert way to the temple, removed lest the Assyrian covet the sacred vessels (Jarchi)

schools.summary: Gill: the fuller acts of Ahaz survive in Chronicles and in the annals of the kings of Judah, now lost.
Ellicott notes the variant fuller formula; Keil and Delitzsch make the closing formula the doorway to the verdict of dishonourable burial.
Gill: buried in David's city but not in the royal tombs, being so wicked a prince.
Clarke: the burial a mark of degradation; the worst king yet, with every advantage and without excuse — he would sin, and so must suffer.
Keil and Delitzsch record plainly the succession of Hezekiah.
Matthew Henry: Ahaz dies mid-life and leaves the throne to a better man — Hezekiah, a brand plucked from the burning.
